title: Glacial Landscapes — How Ice Shaped the British Uplands
examTip: AQA Geography Paper 1, Topic 3 (Physical Landscapes in the UK). Expect questions on glacial processes, formation of upland erosional landforms (corrie, arête, pyramidal peak, U-shaped valley), depositional landforms, and economic uses of glaciated areas.
introTips: How glaciers erode, transport, and deposit material to shape landscapes
How to explain the formation of key erosional and depositional glacial landforms step by step
The economic opportunities that glaciated landscapes like the Lake District offer today
intro: Walk through the Lake District, Snowdonia, or the Scottish Highlands and you're walking through a landscape carved by ice. Everything — the dramatic corries, the knife-edged ridges, the wide U-shaped valleys, the lakes — was shaped during the last Ice Age, when glaciers covered much of Britain. These landscapes now attract millions of tourists, support farming and water supplies, and generate renewable energy. Understanding how ice created them is the foundation for understanding why they matter today.

keyPoints.heading: Glacial processes — how ice erodes, moves, and deposits
keyPoints.content: Freeze-thaw weathering weakens rock before the glacier even touches it: water enters cracks, freezes and expands by 9%, widening the crack; repeated freezing and thawing eventually fragments rock — producing scree (angular rock debris). Most effective where temperature fluctuates around 0°C. Abrasion: rocks and debris frozen into the base of the glacier act like coarse sandpaper, scratching and grinding the bedrock beneath as the glacier moves. This leaves striations (scratches) in the rock and produces very fine sediment called rock flour. Plucking: meltwater at the glacier's base seeps into cracks in the bedrock and refreezes, bonding to the glacier; as the glacier moves forward it rips out chunks of rock — particularly effective on well-jointed rocks like granite. Rotational slip: in a corrie, the glacier rotates as it moves, increasing erosion at the base of the bowl. Glaciers move by two mechanisms: basal sliding (meltwater lubricates the base, allowing the glacier to slide) and internal deformation (ice crystals slide over each other within the glacier body).
writeBullets: Freeze-thaw — water in cracks freezes, expands 9%, widens crack; produces scree
Abrasion — rocks in glacier base grind bedrock; leaves striations; produces rock flour
Plucking — meltwater refreezes round rock, glacier rips it out; effective on jointed granite
Rotational slip — glacier rotates in corrie, deepening the floor
Basal sliding — meltwater lubricates base; glacier slides forward
Internal deformation — ice crystals slip over each other within glacier body

keyTerms.def: Water freezes in rock cracks, expands by 9%, widening cracks until fragments break off as scree
Grinding and scratching of bedrock by rocks frozen into the glacier base — leaves striations and produces rock flour
Meltwater freezing around bedrock joints, then being ripped out as the glacier moves
The rotating movement of a glacier in a corrie, deepening the floor through abrasion
Very fine sediment produced by glacial abrasion — often gives glacial lakes a distinctive milky colour
Scratches on bedrock caused by rocks dragged along in the glacier base — indicate direction of glacier movement

keyPoints.memTip: Glacial erosion processes: Freeze-thaw, Abrasion, Plucking, Rotational slip — FAPR ('Frost And Plucking Rock'). Remember: abrasion leaves striations; plucking leaves jagged rock faces. Cover your notes and explain each process with its mechanism from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse abrasion and plucking. Abrasion = smoothing and scratching (like sandpaper). Plucking = ripping out chunks (like pulling up a floorboard). Abrasion leaves smooth, striated surfaces; plucking leaves rough, jagged surfaces. Both can appear in a 4-mark formation question.

keyPoints.heading: Erosional upland landforms — corrie, arête, and pyramidal peak
keyPoints.content: The most dramatic glacial landforms are found in mountain areas where glaciers originated. A corrie (also called cwm in Wales or cirque in France) is an armchair-shaped hollow on a mountainside. It forms where snow accumulates in a hollow, compacts into ice, and begins to move. Rotational slip deepens the floor by abrasion; plucking steepens the back wall; freeze-thaw shatters rock on the back wall. The front lip is less deeply eroded (glacier was thinner and moving faster there). When the ice melts, the hollow often fills with water forming a tarn. UK examples: Red Tarn (under Helvellyn, Lake District), Glaslyn (Snowdon, Wales). An arête is a knife-edge ridge formed where two corries on opposite sides of a mountain erode backward toward each other, removing the mountain between them and leaving a narrow, jagged ridge. Examples: Striding Edge (Helvellyn) and Crib Goch (Snowdon). A pyramidal peak forms when three or more corries erode on all sides of a mountain, leaving a sharp, pointed summit. The world's most famous example is the Matterhorn (Switzerland/Italy). In the UK, Ben Nevis has some pyramidal characteristics.
writeBullets: Corrie — armchair hollow; rotational slip deepens floor, plucking steepens back wall, freeze-thaw shatters rock
Tarn — mountain lake that forms in a corrie hollow when ice melts (e.g., Red Tarn, Helvellyn)
Arête — knife-edge ridge where two corries erode back to back (e.g., Striding Edge, Helvellyn)
Pyramidal peak — 3+ corries erode all sides; sharp pointed summit (e.g., Matterhorn, Switzerland)
Corrie lip — less eroded at front; glacier was thinner and moving faster there
UK examples — Red Tarn and Glaslyn (corries); Striding Edge and Crib Goch (arêtes)

keyTerms.def: An armchair-shaped hollow on a mountainside where a glacier originated — often contains a tarn (mountain lake)
A mountain lake occupying a corrie hollow after the glacier melted
A knife-edge ridge formed when two corries erode back-to-back, removing the material between them
A sharp mountain summit formed when three or more corries erode from all sides — e.g., the Matterhorn
A famous pyramidal peak on the Swiss-Italian border — formed by three corries eroding from different sides

keyPoints.memTip: Corrie → Arête → Pyramidal peak is a progression: one hollow → two hollows erode toward each other (arête between them) → three or more hollows on all sides (pyramidal peak). Think C-A-P (Corrie, Arête, Peak) like a mountain cap. Cover your notes and explain all three formation sequences from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Formation of a corrie is the most commonly examined upland landform. Make sure you mention rotational slip (deepens floor), plucking (steepens back wall), freeze-thaw (shatters back wall), and the corrie lip (less erosion at the front). Use Red Tarn or Glaslyn as your UK example.

keyPoints.heading: Valley landforms — U-shaped valleys, hanging valleys, and ribbon lakes
keyPoints.content: As glaciers flow downhill, they transform pre-existing river valleys into distinctively different shapes. A glacial trough (U-shaped valley) forms as a glacier deepens and widens a former V-shaped river valley. The glacier erodes by abrasion and plucking, producing a broad, flat floor and very steep, straight sides. Examples: Nant Ffrancon (Snowdonia), Borrowdale (Lake District), Lauterbrunnen Valley (Switzerland). Truncated spurs: the interlocking spurs of the original river valley are cut off by the glacier (which, unlike a river, cannot easily wind around obstacles), leaving flat-faced, cliff-like triangular shapes on the valley sides. Hanging valleys: tributary glaciers were much smaller than the main valley glacier and could not erode as deeply. When all the ice melts, the tributary valley is left perched high up on the main valley side — often marked by a dramatic waterfall where it drops to the main valley floor. Examples: Yosemite Valley (California), Cwm Idwal (Snowdonia). Ribbon lakes: long, narrow lakes filling rock basins on glacial trough floors — rock was softer there and was eroded more deeply. Examples: Windermere, Ullswater, and Thirlmere (Lake District).
writeBullets: U-shaped valley/glacial trough — glacier widens and deepens V-shaped valley; flat floor, steep sides (e.g., Nant Ffrancon, Borrowdale)
Truncated spurs — interlocking spurs cut off by glacier; flat-faced triangular cliffs on valley sides
Hanging valley — small tributary glacier couldn't erode as deep; left perched above main valley; waterfall drops to floor
Ribbon lake — long narrow lake in rock basin on valley floor; soft rock eroded more deeply (e.g., Windermere, Ullswater)
Contrast with river valley — glacier unable to wind round obstacles; cuts straight through spurs
UK examples — Borrowdale, Nant Ffrancon (troughs); Windermere, Ullswater, Thirlmere (ribbon lakes)

keyTerms.def: A U-shaped valley carved by a glacier — broad, flat floor and steep, straight sides
A former interlocking spur cut off by a glacier, leaving a flat-faced cliff on the valley side
A tributary valley left high above the main valley floor because the tributary glacier was smaller and couldn't erode as deeply
A long, narrow lake on a glacial trough floor, occupying a rock basin where soft rock was eroded more deeply

keyPoints.memTip: Valley landforms: U-shaped valley (ice widens and deepens), Truncated spurs (spurs sliced off), Hanging valley (small glacier left high up), Ribbon lake (soft rock basin filled with water). Think UTHR — 'Under The Hanging Ribbon'. Cover your notes and name a UK example for each landform from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The comparison between V-shaped and U-shaped valleys is a classic exam question. V-shape: river, vertical erosion, winds around hard rock, interlocking spurs. U-shape: glacier, abrasion and plucking on all sides, cuts through spurs, flat floor, steep sides. Know both sides of that comparison.

keyPoints.heading: Depositional landforms and the economic value of glaciated landscapes
keyPoints.content: Till is unsorted, unstratified sediment deposited directly by glacier ice — a mixed jumble of all particle sizes from clay to boulders. Moraines are ridges of till: lateral moraine (debris from valley sides, deposited along glacier edges), medial moraine (where two glaciers merge, their lateral moraines combine into a central ridge), terminal moraine (a ridge at the furthest point of glacier advance — marks how far the ice reached), and ground moraine (till spread across the valley floor as the glacier retreated). Drumlins are streamlined hills of till, shaped like an upturned spoon — blunt end facing upstream (stoss side), tapering downstream (lee side). They occur in swarms, creating distinctive 'basket of eggs' topography. Examples: Ribble Valley (Lancashire), Eden Valley (Cumbria). Erratics are boulders of a rock type entirely different from the local geology, transported far from their source by glaciers and deposited when the ice melted. Example: Norber erratics (Yorkshire Dales — Silurian slate boulders resting on Carboniferous limestone). Economic uses of glaciated landscapes: tourism (Lake District — 19.7 million visitors/year; Snowdonia; Cairngorms — walking, climbing, skiing); farming (fertile glacial soils in valley floors for dairy farming — Cumbria's dairy industry); HEP (steep valleys and reliable water supply ideal — several HEP stations in Snowdonia); reservoirs (flooded glacial valleys supply urban areas — Thirlmere and Haweswater supply Manchester).
writeBullets: Till — unsorted, unstratified sediment deposited by glacier; mix of all particle sizes
Terminal moraine — ridge at furthest advance of glacier; marks maximum extent of ice
Drumlins — streamlined till hills; blunt end upstream, tapered downstream; 'basket of eggs' (e.g., Ribble Valley)
Erratics — boulders transported far from source; different rock type to local geology (e.g., Norber, Yorkshire Dales)
Tourism — Lake District 19.7m visitors/year; Snowdonia; Scottish Highlands
Reservoirs — glacial valleys flooded; Thirlmere and Haweswater supply Manchester

keyTerms.def: Unstratified, unsorted sediment deposited directly by glacier ice — a mixture of all particle sizes
A ridge of till deposited at the furthest point of glacier advance — marks the maximum extent of the ice
A streamlined hill of till, blunt upstream and tapered downstream — formed beneath moving ice
A boulder of a different rock type to the local geology, transported by a glacier from its source
A ridge of debris deposited along the sides of a glacier from material falling from valley walls

keyPoints.memTip: Depositional landforms: Till (random deposit), Moraines (ridges — lateral, medial, terminal, ground), Drumlins (streamlined mounds), Erratics (foreign boulders). Economic uses: Tourism, Farming, HEP, Reservoirs — TFHR. Cover your notes and name two depositional landforms and two economic uses with specific examples from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Economic use questions are often extended writing (6 marks). Don't just list — explain why the glacial landscape makes each use possible. Tourism: dramatic scenery created by glaciation (corries, ridges, lakes). Reservoirs: glacial valleys are deep, steep-sided, and receive high rainfall — perfect shape for a reservoir.

tables.title: Glacial erosional landforms — process and UK example
rows: Rotational slip (deepens floor), plucking (steepens back wall), freeze-thaw (shatters back wall)
Two corries erode back-to-back, removing the mountain between them
Three or more corries erode on all sides of a mountain
Abrasion and plucking deepen and widen a V-shaped river valley
Glacier cuts through interlocking spurs, leaving flat-faced cliff on valley side
Common in Snowdonia and Lake District
Tributary glacier smaller, unable to erode as deeply; left above main valley
Cwm Idwal, Snowdonia; many in Yosemite, California
Soft rock eroded more deeply by glacier; basin fills with water after ice melts

tables.title: Economic uses of glaciated upland landscapes
rows: Dramatic scenery — corries, arêtes, U-valleys, tarns attract visitors
High relief and snow in winter; glacially carved bowls suit ski runs
Fertile alluvium and till in flat-floored glacial valleys; reliable rainfall
Cumbria (Lake District valleys) — major milk producer
Steep valley sides and reliable water supply ideal for HEP stations
Deep, steep-sided glacial valleys ideal reservoir shape; high rainfall catchment
Thirlmere and Haweswater → Manchester water supply

0.question: Which glacial erosion process involves meltwater freezing around jointed bedrock and the glacier ripping it out as it moves?
0.correctFeedback: Correct. Plucking occurs when meltwater at the base of the glacier seeps into cracks in the bedrock and refreezes, bonding the rock to the glacier. As the glacier moves forward, it wrenches out chunks of rock — leaving a jagged, rough surface.
0.wrongFeedback: Abrasion is when rocks frozen into the glacier base grind and scratch the bedrock (like sandpaper), leaving striations. Rotational slip is the rotating movement of ice in a corrie that deepens the floor. Freeze-thaw weathering involves water in cracks freezing and expanding to fragment rock above ground. Plucking is specifically the process where meltwater refreezes around bedrock and the moving glacier rips the rock out.

1.question: What is a tarn and how does it form?
options: A lake on a wide floodplain formed by a meandering river being cut off
A mountain lake that occupies a corrie hollow after the glacier melted
A long narrow lake occupying a rock basin eroded on a glacial trough floor
A lake formed behind a terminal moraine when meltwater is trapped
1.correctFeedback: Right. A tarn is the lake that forms in the hollow of a corrie after the glacier melts. The corrie's lip (which is less deeply eroded) acts as a natural dam, holding the water in the bowl-shaped hollow.
1.wrongFeedback: A tarn is the specific name for a mountain lake that forms in a corrie (cirque/cwm) after the ice melts. The lip at the front of the corrie — which was less eroded because the glacier was thinner and faster there — acts as a natural barrier, trapping meltwater. Red Tarn under Helvellyn in the Lake District is a classic example. Ribbon lakes (like Windermere) are different — they're long narrow lakes on valley floors.

3.question: What is an erratic and what does its presence tell geographers?
options: A very large drumlin that formed under particularly fast-moving ice
A boulder of different rock type to the local geology, transported by a glacier from a distant source
A ridge of unsorted sediment deposited at the furthest point of glacier advance
A striated rock surface showing the direction of glacier movement
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. An erratic is a boulder of rock type that doesn't match the local geology — it was picked up by a glacier far away and deposited when the ice melted. Geographers can trace the glacier's path by comparing the rock type of the erratic to possible source areas.
3.wrongFeedback: An erratic is a boulder deposited by a glacier far from where it originated — its rock type is completely different from the bedrock beneath it. The Norber erratics in the Yorkshire Dales are dark Silurian slate boulders resting on pale Carboniferous limestone. Since Silurian slate outcrops exist only some distance away, geographers can trace exactly which direction the glacier that carried them must have come from. Erratics are powerful evidence for past glaciation.

commonMistakes: Confusing abrasion (smoothing and scratching bedrock) with plucking (ripping out chunks) — abrasion leaves striations; plucking leaves rough, jagged faces
Describing a corrie formation without all three processes — you need rotational slip (deepens floor), plucking (steepens back wall), AND freeze-thaw (shatters back wall)
Saying ribbon lakes are formed in the same way as tarns — they're not; ribbon lakes are on valley floors in soft rock basins; tarns are in corrie hollows on mountainsides
Not explaining WHY hanging valleys are 'hanging' — it's because the tributary glacier was smaller and couldn't erode as deeply as the main glacier
Forgetting to link economic uses back to glaciation — explain HOW the glaciated landscape makes each use possible, not just what the use is
examTips: Corrie formation is the most frequently examined upland landform — write out the full sequence from memory: snow accumulates → compacts to ice → rotational slip → abrasion deepens floor → plucking steepens back wall → freeze-thaw shatters rock → corrie lip at front → tarn when ice melts
Extended economic use questions (6 marks) expect you to explain HOW the glacial landscape enables each use — steep sides, ribbon lakes, dramatic scenery — not just name the uses
Distinguish till (unsorted, deposited by ice) from fluvioglacial deposits (sorted by meltwater streams) — AQA can ask you to compare them
revisionCardBullets: Glacial erosion: Freeze-thaw (expands 9%), Abrasion (striations, rock flour), Plucking (meltwater refreezes, rips rock out), Rotational slip (deepens corrie floor)
Corrie: armchair hollow; rotational slip, plucking, freeze-thaw; tarn in hollow (Red Tarn, Helvellyn)
Arête: knife-edge ridge from two back-to-back corries (Striding Edge, Helvellyn)
Pyramidal peak: 3+ corries on all sides (Matterhorn, Switzerland)
U-shaped valley: glacier deepens and widens V-valley; flat floor, steep sides (Nant Ffrancon, Borrowdale)
Truncated spurs: interlocking spurs cut off by glacier; flat-faced cliffs on valley sides
Hanging valley: small tributary glacier left perched above main valley floor; waterfall drops down
Ribbon lake: long narrow lake in soft rock basin on valley floor (Windermere, Ullswater, Thirlmere)
Till: unsorted, unstratified sediment deposited by ice
Terminal moraine: ridge at furthest glacier advance; Drumlins: streamlined till hills, blunt upstream; Erratics: foreign boulders (Norber, Yorkshire Dales)
Economic uses: Tourism (19.7m visitors/year, Lake District), dairy farming (Cumbria), HEP (Dinorwig, Snowdonia), Reservoirs (Thirlmere → Manchester)
Basket of eggs: drumlin swarm landscape — e.g., Ribble Valley, Lancashire
